title: Her Letter
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½m sitting alone by the fire,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dressed just as I came from the dance,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In a robe even you would admire,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It cost a cool thousand in France;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½m be-diamonded out of all reason,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My hair is done up in a cue:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In short, sir, ן¿½the belle of the seasonן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Is wasting an hour upon you.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A dozen engagements Iן¿½ve broken;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I left in the midst of a set;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Likewise a proposal, half spoken,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That waits on the stairs for me yet.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They say heן¿½ll be rich, when he grows up,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And then he adores me indeed;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And you, sir, are turning your nose up,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Three thousand miles off as you read.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And how do I like my position?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And what do I think of New York?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And now, in my higher ambition,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With whom do I waltz, flirt, or talk?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And isnן¿½t it nice to have riches,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And diamonds and silks, and all that?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And arenן¿½t they a change to the ditches
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And tunnels of Poverty Flat?ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Well, yes, if you saw us out driving
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Each day in the Park, four-in-hand,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If you saw poor dear mamma contriving
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To look supernaturally grand,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If you saw papaן¿½s picture, as taken
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By Brady, and tinted at that,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Youן¿½d never suspect he sold bacon
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And flour at Poverty Flat.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And yet, just this moment, when sitting
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the glare of the grand chandelier,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the bustle and glitter befitting
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The ן¿½finest soiree of the year,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the mists of a gaze de Chambery,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the hum of the smallest of talk,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Somehow, Joe, I thought of the ן¿½Ferry,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the dance that we had on ן¿½The Fork;ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of Harrisonן¿½s barn, with its muster
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of flags festooned over the wall;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of the candles that shed their soft lustre
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And tallow on head-dress and shawl;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of the steps that we took to one fiddle,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of the dress of my queer vis-a-vis;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And how I once went down the middle
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With the man that shot Sandy McGee;

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of the moon that was quietly sleeping
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the hill, when the time came to go;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of the few baby peaks that were peeping
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From under their bedclothes of snow;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of that ride that to me was the rarest;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of the something you said at the gate.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ah! Joe, then I wasnן¿½t an heiress
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To ן¿½the best-paying lead in the State.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Well, well, itן¿½s all past; yet itן¿½s funny
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To think, as I stood in the glare
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of fashion and beauty and money,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That I should be thinking, right there,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of some one who breasted high water,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And swam the North Fork, and all that,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Just to dance with old Folinsbeeן¿½s daughter,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Lily of Poverty Flat.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But goodness! what nonsense Iן¿½m writing!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Mamma says my taste still is low),
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Instead of my triumphs reciting,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½m spooning on Joseph, heigh-ho!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Iן¿½m to be ן¿½finishedן¿½ by travel,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whateverן¿½s the meaning of that.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh, why did papa strike pay gravel
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In drifting on Poverty Flat?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Good-night! hereן¿½s the end of my paper;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Good-night! if the longitude please,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For maybe, while wasting my taper,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Your sunן¿½s climbing over the trees.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But know, if you havenן¿½t got riches,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And are poor, dearest Joe, and all that,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That my heartן¿½s somewhere there in the ditches,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And youן¿½ve struck it, on Poverty Flat.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Bret Harte (Francis) represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Bret Harte (Francis)'s bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
